1: Solomon's purpose: to build a house for YHWH's name and a palace for himself. The Name-theology — the temple is the dwelling of the šēm — is the OT framework for divine presence mediated through a representative location. Christ makes the Name-theology personal: 'I have made your name known to them' (John 17:26); he is the Name-bearer in whom the fullness of YHWH's character dwells (Colossians 1:19).
2: 70,000 burden-bearers and 80,000 stone-cutters conscripted for the building work. The massive labor force reflects the magnitude of the building project. Christ builds his church (Matthew 16:18) through willing servants, the stones being believers themselves: 'like living stones, you are being built up as a spiritual house' (1 Peter 2:5).
3: The letter to Huram of Tyre — Gentile international cooperation for the temple project. The temple's construction required Gentile participation — an OT anticipation of Gentile inclusion in the eschatological temple: 'foreigners who join themselves to the LORD... these I will bring to my holy mountain' (Isaiah 56:6-7; Ephesians 2:19-22).
4: The temple's purpose: to burn fragrant incense, offer continual bread, present burnt offerings morning and evening, Sabbaths and appointed feasts. The perpetual worship rhythms find their fulfillment in Christ, who 'always lives to make intercession' (Hebrews 7:25) and in the new creation worship where there is no night (Revelation 22:5).
5: 'The house that I am to build will be great, for our God is greater than all gods.' The temple's greatness is derived from YHWH's incomparability. But the greater temple is Christ's body: 'something greater than the temple is here' (Matthew 12:6). All the temple's greatness was pointing toward the greater-than-Solomon who would be the true dwelling place of deity.
6: 'Who is able to build him a house, since heaven, even highest heaven, cannot contain him?' The temple's construction is preceded by its own impossible paradox — YHWH cannot be housed. Stephen quotes this logic (Acts 7:48-49) to show that the temple was never a final solution. The paradox resolves in Christ: 'in him the whole fullness of deity dwells bodily' (Colossians 2:9) — not in a building but in a Person.
7: Solomon requests a skilled craftsman — a Bezalel-figure trained in all materials and engraving. The spirit-endowed craftsman who serves the temple is the OT type of the Spirit-gifted builder in the NT: 'to each is given the manifestation of the Spirit for the common good' (1 Corinthians 12:7) — every gift directed toward building the temple that is the body of Christ (Ephesians 4:12-16).
8: Timber from Lebanon — cedar, cypress, and algum. The best materials from the whole known world gathered to build YHWH's house. The eschatological temple in Rev 21 is built from imperishable materials — gold, jasper, precious stones — because its builder is God (Hebrews 11:10).
9: 'The house I am to build will be great and wonderful.' Christ is the fulfillment of divine wonder-building: 'we beheld his glory, glory as of the only Son from the Father, full of grace and truth' (John 1:14). The temple's wonder was a reflected glory; the incarnation is the direct radiance of the Father's glory (Hebrews 1:3).
10: The wages for Tyrian workers: wheat, barley, wine, and oil. Christ provides not commodities but himself: 'I am the bread of life' (John 6:35), 'I am the true vine' (John 15:1) — the temple-builder who feeds those who labor in his service with the substance of his own person.
11: Huram's response: 'Because the LORD loves his people, he has made you king over them.' A Gentile king's acknowledgment that Israel's king stands under divine favor. The nations' recognition of YHWH's love for Israel is the eschatological pattern: 'all the nations shall come and worship before you' (Revelation 15:4), acknowledging the LORD's righteous acts.
12: Huram praises 'the LORD God of Israel, who made heaven and earth.' The Gentile king confesses the Creator — a remarkable acknowledgment from outside the covenant. Paul makes this the basis of universal accountability (Romans 1:20) and the ground of mission (Acts 17:24). Christ is the one through whom all things were made (Colossians 1:16); Gentile praise of the Creator finds its fullest form in worship of the Son through whom creation came.
13: Huram-abi is sent — a skilled craftsman who mirrors Bezalel in skill: engraving, carpentry, metalwork. The craftsman who bridges ethnic boundaries to serve the temple is a type of the Spirit-gifted servant in the one body 'where there is not Greek and Jew, circumcised and uncircumcised' (Colossians 3:11) — all serving the building up of the one temple.
14: Huram-abi's mixed heritage — his mother from Dan/Naphtali in Israel, his father Tyrian — echoes Bezalel (Judah) and Oholiab (Dan) working together on the tabernacle. The body of Christ is built from every tribe and tongue (Revelation 7:9; 1 Corinthians 12:12-14), each contributing what the Spirit has given.
15: The Tyrians will send timber to Joppa; Solomon will carry it to Jerusalem. The new creation's building project draws from 'the glory and honor of the nations' being brought into it (Revelation 21:26) — the entire harvest of human achievement gathered into the city whose builder and architect is God.
16: Cedar rafted from Lebanon to Joppa (Yafo) — the port that will later receive Peter's vision of Gentile inclusion (Acts 10). The same harbor where Gentile timber arrives for the temple becomes the place where the vision of Gentile participation in God's people is given. Sacred geography carries typological freight across the biblical narrative.
17: The census of aliens in the land — 153,600 non-Israelites conscripted for the building project. Aliens building YHWH's house prefigures the eschatological reality: 'you are no longer strangers and aliens, but you are fellow citizens with the saints and members of the household of God, built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets, Christ Jesus himself being the cornerstone' (Ephesians 2:19-20).
18: Distribution of labor: 70,000 burden-bearers, 80,000 stone-cutters, 3,600 overseers. The organized workforce for temple-building. Christ appoints diverse gifts for building his church — apostles, prophets, evangelists, pastors, teachers (Ephesians 4:11-12) — each with a specific function in the construction project that continues 'until we all attain to the unity of the faith' (Ephesians 4:13).
